era_context: Continuation of Egypt's Fourth Dynasty and Mesopotamia's Early Dynastic Period III. The Indus Valley Civilisation remains in its mature phase. This year falls within the era of monumental pyramid construction in Egypt and competitive city-state politics in Mesopotamia. Writing systems are well-established across the Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus Valley

events.title: Reign of Menkaure, Fourth Dynasty Egypt
events.description: Menkaure (also Mycerinus) is traditionally dated to reign during this period as the fifth or sixth pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty. He commissioned the construction of the third-largest pyramid at Giza and is associated with a period of continued centralised power and monumental construction. His reign marks the later phase of the Fourth Dynasty's pyramid-building programme. Conventional dating places his reign circa 2532–2503 BCE, but this is subject to the same chronological uncertainties as his predecessors.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza pyramid complex

events.certainty_note: Same chronological uncertainties as Khafre. The conventional dating is circa 2532–2503 BCE, but alternative chronologies could shift this by 50–100 years. The assignment to -3023 is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional chronology of Menkaure's reign is subject to the same criticisms as that of Khafre. Low chronologies would place this reign earlier. Additionally, the succession of Fourth Dynasty pharaohs is not entirely certain; some scholars debate whether Menkaure was the direct successor of Khafre or whether other rulers intervened.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -3023 is sparse and indirect. The year is defined almost entirely by the conventional chronology of Egyptian royal succession, which is based on later king lists and archaeological evidence rather than contemporary records. Mesopotamian sources for this specific year are absent. The record is heavily weighted toward elite Egyptian activities.
